task: Design a token-bucket rate limiter for an HTTP API that allows 100 requests per second per API key, running across a single multi-threaded process. Describe the data structures, the algorithm for refilling and consuming tokens, and the exact synchronization strategy. Assume thousands of concurrent worker threads may call into the limiter for the same key simultaneously. Provide enough detail that an engineer could implement it correctly.
constraints: Specify the exact locking or atomic strategy and explain why a naive read-modify-write of the token count is unsafe under concurrency
Address what happens when many threads check-and-decrement the same key's bucket at the same instant, ensuring the bucket never goes negative and the limit is never exceeded
Explain how token refill is computed from elapsed time without a background thread, avoiding drift or burst-on-idle errors
Address per-key map growth/eviction so inactive keys do not leak memory

task: Design an in-memory cache with per-entry TTL expiration for a service handling heavy concurrent reads and writes. Reads should be fast and lock-free where possible. Describe how entries expire, how stale entries are reclaimed, and how you prevent the 'cache stampede' / thundering-herd problem when a popular key expires and many threads simultaneously try to recompute it. Give concrete data structures and synchronization details.
constraints: Describe the cache-stampede mitigation precisely (e.g. single-flight / per-key recompute lock) so only one thread recomputes an expired hot key while others wait or serve stale
Address the race between one thread reading an entry and another expiring/evicting it, ensuring no use-after-free or returning a half-written value
Explain the reclamation strategy for expired entries (lazy on access vs active sweep) and its memory implications
Specify how TTL is checked relative to a clock and the effect of clock skew or non-monotonic time

task: Design a bounded multi-producer multi-consumer work queue used by a thread pool: producers enqueue jobs, N worker threads dequeue and run them, and the system must support graceful shutdown that finishes in-flight jobs but stops accepting new ones. Describe the queue, the blocking/signaling mechanism, and the shutdown protocol in enough detail to implement.
constraints: Address the lost-wakeup / missed-signal race between a producer signaling and a consumer about to block, and how condition-variable predicates are re-checked under lock
Explain how graceful shutdown avoids both deadlock (workers blocked waiting on an empty queue that will never fill) and dropped jobs already enqueued
Specify behavior when the bounded queue is full: blocking vs rejection, and how that interacts with shutdown
Ensure no job is executed twice and no worker spins busy-waiting

task: Review the design of a 'fetch a URL on the user's behalf' feature: users submit a URL, the backend issues an HTTP GET to it and returns the response body (used for link previews). The current plan validates that the URL starts with http:// or https:// and rejects anything containing 'localhost' or '127.0.0.1'. Identify the security problems with this design and specify a robust mitigation.
constraints: Identify this as SSRF and enumerate bypasses the string check misses (e.g. alternate IP encodings, IPv6, 0.0.0.0, link-local 169.254.169.254 cloud metadata, DNS names resolving to private IPs)
Address the DNS-rebinding / TOCTOU gap between validating a hostname and actually connecting, and how to close it (resolve-then-pin or validate the connected IP)
Address redirect following, since a public URL can 302 to an internal address
Specify an all-list/deny approach on resolved IP ranges plus protocol, port, and response-size limits rather than substring matching

task: Design password reset for a web application. A user enters their email, receives a reset link, clicks it, and sets a new password. Specify the full token lifecycle and the endpoints involved, with security as the priority. Provide enough detail to implement.
constraints: Specify that the reset token is high-entropy, single-use, and short-lived, and that only a hash of it is stored server-side (not the raw token)
Address account enumeration: the 'enter email' response must not reveal whether an account exists
Require invalidating existing sessions and any outstanding reset tokens once a reset completes, and constant-time/hashed token lookup
Address rate limiting and the risk of the token leaking via the HTTP Referer header or logs

task: Review a multi-tenant SaaS REST API where every record belongs to a tenant. Authentication uses a signed JWT containing user_id and tenant_id. An engineer proposes that each endpoint reads the resource by its ID from the URL (e.g. GET /invoices/{id}) and returns it if the JWT signature is valid. Identify the authorization flaws and specify the correct access-control design.
constraints: Identify the IDOR / broken-object-level-authorization flaw: a valid token for tenant A can read tenant B's resource by guessing/iterating IDs
Require that every data access be scoped/filtered by the authenticated tenant_id at the query level, not just signature-checked
Address trusting claims inside the JWT (e.g. a client-supplied tenant_id or role) versus verifying them server-side, and token expiry/revocation
Address enumerable sequential IDs and information leakage via differing 403 vs 404 responses

task: Design an idempotent 'create payment' API endpoint. Clients may retry on timeout or network failure, and the same logical request must never charge a customer twice, even if two retries arrive concurrently. Describe the use of an idempotency key, the storage model, and the request lifecycle in enough detail to implement against a relational database.
constraints: Explain how the idempotency key is recorded and how a concurrent duplicate is detected and made to wait or return the original result, not double-charge (address the race where two retries arrive at the same instant)
Specify what is returned for a retry of an in-progress vs already-completed request, including the stored response
Address the failure window where the charge succeeded at the payment processor but the local record was not yet committed, and how recovery reconciles it
Define key scope/expiry and what happens if a client reuses a key with a different request body

task: Design an at-least-once message consumer that processes events from a queue and writes results to a database, where the queue may redeliver a message if the consumer crashes before acknowledging. The processing includes both a DB write and a call to an external email service. Make the end-to-end effect safe under redelivery. Provide implementation-level detail.
constraints: Explain how duplicate processing is made idempotent for the DB write (e.g. dedup key / upsert keyed on message id) so redelivery does not double-apply
Address the dual-write problem: the DB commit and the email send cannot be made atomic, so specify ordering and the outbox/relay or dedup approach to avoid lost or duplicated side effects
Address ack ordering: acknowledging before vs after the work completes, and the consequence of a crash in each window
Address poison messages that always fail, with a dead-letter / max-retry strategy

task: Design a leader-elected scheduled-job runner: several identical service instances run, but a given cron job must execute on exactly one instance per scheduled tick. Engineers propose using a database row as a lock that an instance grabs to 'become leader' and then runs all jobs. Critique this and design the coordination so a job is not skipped or run twice across instances. Provide enough detail to implement.
constraints: Address lock expiry / fencing: what happens if the leader pauses (GC, VM freeze) past its lease and a second instance also becomes leader, causing concurrent execution
Explain how a job tick is uniquely claimed (e.g. unique constraint on job_id+scheduled_time) so duplicate runs are impossible even with two leaders
Address clock skew between instances affecting when a tick fires
Address a missed tick when the leader dies mid-execution, and how the job is recovered without double-running already-completed work

task: You maintain a public JSON REST API consumed by thousands of third-party clients you do not control. You need to evolve an endpoint that currently returns a user object with a single 'name' field into one that supports separate 'first_name' and 'last_name', and to change a 'status' field from a free-form string to a fixed enum. Specify a backward-compatible migration plan and the contract rules going forward.
constraints: Guarantee existing clients that read 'name' and arbitrary 'status' strings keep working (no removed/renamed fields, additive changes, tolerant reader / never-break-on-unknown-enum guidance)
Address how new enum values are introduced without breaking old clients that don't recognize them, and how clients should handle unknown values
Specify a versioning/deprecation strategy (when to bump a version vs evolve in place) and a sunset timeline communicated to clients
Address default values and null handling so old and new fields stay mutually consistent during the transition

task: Design a cursor-based pagination scheme for an API listing items ordered by creation time, where items can be inserted and deleted while a client paginates. Explain the cursor format, the query, and the ordering guarantees. Provide enough detail to implement against a relational database.
constraints: Explain why offset/limit pagination is unsafe here (items shift, causing skipped or duplicated rows) and how the cursor avoids it
Address the tie-break problem when multiple items share the same creation timestamp, ensuring a stable total ordering (e.g. composite cursor of timestamp + unique id)
Specify behavior when the row a cursor points at is deleted, and how the next page is still computed correctly
Address cursor opacity/tamper-resistance and what happens for out-of-range or stale cursors

task: Design a webhook delivery system: your platform POSTs event notifications to customer-provided HTTPS endpoints. Specify the delivery contract, retry behavior, and how receivers can trust and safely process the events. Provide implementation-level detail for both sender and receiver sides.
constraints: Specify a signature scheme (HMAC over the raw body with a shared secret) and require receivers verify it, and address replay protection via a signed timestamp and tolerance window
Address at-least-once delivery: receivers must be idempotent because retries and duplicates will occur, and specify a stable event id for dedup
Specify retry policy with exponential backoff and a cap, and how out-of-order delivery is handled (events may not arrive in causal order)
Address receiver-side safety: verifying signature before parsing, timeouts, and not letting slow receivers stall the whole pipeline

task: A service must detect duplicate records in a stream of ~500 million incoming items per day (each item a ~64-byte key), answering 'have I seen this key recently' with a few hours' lookback, under tight memory limits on a single node. An engineer proposes a hash set of all keys. Critique the choice and design a data structure that meets the memory and throughput requirements, stating the tradeoffs.
constraints: Identify that an exact hash set does not fit in memory at this scale and quantify roughly why, motivating a probabilistic structure (e.g. Bloom / counting / cuckoo filter)
State explicitly the false-positive (and for some structures false-negative) semantics and why that is or isn't acceptable for a dedup use case
Address the 'recently / few hours' time-window requirement, since a plain Bloom filter cannot delete or age out entries (e.g. rotating/segmented filters)
Address sizing: how to pick filter size and hash count for a target false-positive rate at the given cardinality

task: Implement-design 'merge K sorted streams' where K can be large (tens of thousands) and each stream is potentially huge or infinite, producing a single globally sorted output with minimal memory. An engineer proposes concatenating all data and calling a standard sort. Critique this and specify the correct approach with its complexity.
constraints: Reject the concatenate-then-sort approach for unbounded/huge streams and justify with memory and the impossibility of sorting infinite input
Specify a k-way merge using a min-heap keyed on the head of each stream, with correct per-step complexity (O(log K) per emitted element) and O(K) memory
Address streams of unequal/empty length and the termination condition, plus tie-breaking for equal keys if stable ordering matters
Address backpressure / what happens when one input stream stalls, so the merge does not block-buffer unboundedly

task: Design the lookup data structure for an autocomplete feature over a dictionary of ~10 million phrases, returning the top-N most-popular completions for a typed prefix with sub-millisecond latency. An engineer proposes scanning the list and filtering by prefix on each keystroke. Critique this and specify a structure and ranking approach that scales.
constraints: Reject linear scan per keystroke on latency grounds and specify a prefix structure (trie / compressed radix tree or sorted-array binary search on prefix range)
Address ranking: returning the top-N by popularity for a prefix, not just any matches, and how that ranking is precomputed or maintained (e.g. storing top-N at each node)
Address memory footprint at 10M phrases and the update path when popularity scores or the dictionary change
Address Unicode / normalization and case-folding so equivalent inputs match, and degenerate prefixes (very short prefix matching millions of entries)

task: A payment-processing service intermittently loses ~0.1% of transactions with no obvious errors in production. The current logging writes a single 'transaction processed' line on success and swallows exceptions to avoid crashing. Design the observability and error-handling overhaul needed to diagnose and prevent silent transaction loss. Provide concrete, implementation-level guidance.
constraints: Identify that swallowing exceptions hides the failures and specify proper error propagation/handling and structured logging of failures with context, never empty catch blocks
Specify correlation/trace IDs propagated end-to-end so a single transaction can be followed across services, plus logging at each state transition (not only on success)
Specify metrics (counters for attempted vs succeeded vs failed, with alerting on the discrepancy) and how to detect the 0.1% gap automatically rather than by inspection
Address the risk that the observability itself loses data (sampling, dropped logs, async buffer loss) and how to make the success/failure accounting reliable, including idempotency for retried logs

task: Design retry-with-backoff and timeout behavior for a service that calls three downstream dependencies (an auth service, a pricing service, and an inventory service) to serve one request. During a recent incident, a slow downstream caused the whole service to run out of threads and fail all requests, including ones that didn't need that dependency. Design the resilience strategy and the observability to catch this earlier next time.
constraints: Address the resource-exhaustion / cascading-failure cause (threads or connections piling up on a slow dependency) and prescribe timeouts plus bulkheads/isolation so one slow dependency cannot starve the whole service
Specify a circuit breaker and why naive retries make an overloaded dependency worse (retry storm), including jittered exponential backoff and retry budgets
Address per-dependency timeouts that sum within the overall request deadline (deadline propagation), so total latency stays bounded
Specify the metrics/alerts (latency percentiles, error rate, saturation, breaker state per dependency) that would have surfaced the slow dependency before thread exhaustion
